annotation.moveRationale: Black continues playing in a Grünfeld style. prepared by f3 would simply blunder the pawn, so White should either remove one of Black's attackers or prepare it with another piece.
f3 is an Anti-Grünfeld move that was popularized by Alekhine. It prepares e4 with a pawn to secure a big center.
g6, Black commits to a hypermodern development strategy. They will fianchetto their dark-squared bishop with.
White takes more space and increases the control of the important d5 square. This move order allows White's queenside knight to develop to the active c3 square without blocking the c-pawn.
Black controls e4 while developing a knight. This is the most flexible response to 1.
d4 is the Queen's pawn opening. d4 White looks to take over control of the centre.

variations.name: with Nf3

annotation.moveRationale: d5 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the Neo-Grünfeld Defense.

White develops a piece and continues their fight for the central dark squares. This Knight's development, while solid, prevents White's option of playing f3 or f4 and thus gives up options of the aggressive Four Pawns Attack by requiring f4 and the Samisch, which requires f3.

variations.name: Delayed Exchange Variation

annotation.moveRationale: Castles kingside, securing the king and connecting the rooks in the Neo-Grünfeld Defense.
c5 challenges the center from the flank in the Neo-Grünfeld Defense.
Bg2 develops a piece to an active square in the Neo-Grünfeld Defense.
Nxd5 exchanges material, altering the pawn structure in the Neo-Grünfeld Defense.
cxd5 exchanges material, altering the pawn structure in the Neo-Grünfeld Defense.
d5 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the Neo-Grünfeld Defense.
g3 prepares the fianchetto, aiming to place the bishop on a long diagonal in the Neo-Grünfeld Defense.
Castles kingside, securing the king and connecting the rooks in the Neo-Grünfeld Defense.

This Knight development is solid but leaves the options open for Black to either play The King's Indian or the Grunfeld Defence. If Black wishes to play the Grunfeld then responding immediately with d5 is required as white next move is likely to be 5.

Bg7 is the King's Indian Defense, one of the most heavily analysed openings in chess. Black's idea is to concede the center to white and to then put pressure on it using the bishop on g7, often in conjunction with moves like c5 and Nc6.
White intends to play e4 with this move. Developing this knight first also keeps options open for being able to play multiple lines against the King's Indian.
g6, Black commits to a hypermodern development strategy. They will fianchetto their dark-squared bishop with.
White takes more space and increases the control of the important d5 square. This move order allows White's queenside knight to develop to the active c3 square without blocking the c-pawn.
Black controls e4 while developing a knight. This is the most flexible response to 1.
d4 is the Queen's pawn opening. d4 White looks to take over control of the centre.
